cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Prepares for Measuring output with GDP.

core_idea: Scarcity means people want more valuable things than available time, attention, resources, or capacity can provide.
Economics begins here because every choice uses something that could have been used another way.
cards.example: Choosing an evening class means not using that same evening for paid work, sleep, or family time.
cards.key_takeaway: Scarcity turns choice into a real problem.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Scarcity turns choice into a real problem.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Scarcity" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Choosing an evening class means not using that same evening for paid work, sleep, or family time.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Scarcity". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Scarcity

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative you give up when choosing one option.
It makes hidden tradeoffs visible and prevents free-looking choices from being treated as free.
cards.example: A free concert still costs the evening you could have spent studying or resting.
cards.key_takeaway: The true cost of a choice includes the best foregone alternative.

review_prompts.answer_hint: The true cost of a choice includes the best foregone alternative.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Opportunity cost" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A free concert still costs the evening you could have spent studying or resting.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Opportunity cost". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Opportunity cost

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A tradeoff is a tension between desirable goals under a constraint such as time, money, law, or technology.
Good economic reasoning names the binding constraint first; arguing only about which option is preferred, without saying what limits the choice, leaves the actual tradeoff unexplained.
cards.example: A city budget cannot maximize every service at once if revenue and staff are limited.
cards.key_takeaway: Tradeoffs become clearer when the constraint is explicit.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Tradeoffs become clearer when the constraint is explicit.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Tradeoffs and constraints" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A city budget cannot maximize every service at once if revenue and staff are limited.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Tradeoffs and constraints". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Tradeoffs and constraints

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: An incentive is anything that changes the expected benefit, cost, risk, or social meaning of an action.
Incentives matter because people and organizations adjust behavior when rules or payoffs change.
cards.example: A bottle deposit encourages returns by making recycling financially visible at the moment of choice.
cards.key_takeaway: People respond to incentives, including non-money incentives.

review_prompts.answer_hint: People respond to incentives, including non-money incentives.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Incentives" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A bottle deposit encourages returns by making recycling financially visible at the moment of choice.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Incentives". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Incentives

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Marginal thinking compares the extra benefit and extra cost of one more unit or one more step.
It avoids all-or-nothing reasoning because most real decisions happen at the margin, one added unit or step at a time, not as a single yes-or-no verdict on an entire activity.
cards.example: A cafe deciding whether to stay open one extra hour compares extra sales with extra wages and utilities.
cards.key_takeaway: Many choices are about the next increment, not the whole activity.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Many choices are about the next increment, not the whole activity.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Marginal thinking" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A cafe deciding whether to stay open one extra hour compares extra sales with extra wages and utilities.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Marginal thinking". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Marginal thinking

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A sunk cost is a past cost that cannot be recovered and should not decide the next choice by itself.
The hard part is emotional: people often keep investing to avoid admitting an earlier loss.
cards.example: Finishing a bad movie because you paid for the ticket confuses past spending with present value.
cards.key_takeaway: Do not let unrecoverable costs command future decisions.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Do not let unrecoverable costs command future decisions.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Sunk costs" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Finishing a bad movie because you paid for the ticket confuses past spending with present value.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Sunk costs". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Sunk costs

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Comparative advantage means a person or country can gain from specializing where its opportunity cost is lower.
The idea explains why trade can help even when one side is absolutely better at producing everything.
cards.example: A surgeon may hire someone to paint the clinic because surgery time has a higher opportunity cost.
cards.key_takeaway: Trade can create gains by reallocating effort toward lower opportunity costs.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Trade can create gains by reallocating effort toward lower opportunity costs.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Comparative advantage" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A surgeon may hire someone to paint the clinic because surgery time has a higher opportunity cost.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Comparative advantage". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Comparative advantage

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Specialization concentrates effort on narrower tasks so people can build skill, tools, and scale.
It raises productivity but also creates dependence on exchange and coordination.
cards.example: A bakery that focuses on bread can justify better ovens and repeated practice.
cards.key_takeaway: Specialization improves output while increasing interdependence.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Specialization improves output while increasing interdependence.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Specialization" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A bakery that focuses on bread can justify better ovens and repeated practice.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Specialization". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Specialization

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Prepares for Institutions and property rights.

core_idea: A market coordinates many buyers and sellers through prices, rules, information, and property rights.
It is not just a physical place; it is an ongoing process for discovering prices and terms of exchange as buyers and sellers continuously adjust offers to new information.
cards.example: Online ride pricing coordinates drivers, riders, location, and timing without one dispatcher assigning every trip.
cards.key_takeaway: Markets coordinate decentralized plans through signals and rules.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Markets coordinate decentralized plans through signals and rules.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Markets as coordination" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Online ride pricing coordinates drivers, riders, location, and timing without one dispatcher assigning every trip.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Markets as coordination". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Markets as coordination

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Supply describes how much sellers are willing and able to offer at different prices, holding other factors steady.
It reflects costs, technology, expectations, taxes, inputs, and the opportunity cost of producing.
cards.example: If coffee bean costs rise, cafes may offer less coffee at the same price.
cards.key_takeaway: Supply is a schedule of seller behavior, not a single number.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Supply is a schedule of seller behavior, not a single number.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Supply" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If coffee bean costs rise, cafes may offer less coffee at the same price.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Supply". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Supply

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Demand describes how much buyers are willing and able to purchase at different prices, holding other factors steady.
It reflects income, preferences, substitutes, complements, expectations, and the usefulness of the good.
cards.example: If bus fares rise, some commuters may shift to walking, cycling, or carpooling.
cards.key_takeaway: Demand is a relationship between price and quantity buyers choose.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Demand is a relationship between price and quantity buyers choose.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Demand" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If bus fares rise, some commuters may shift to walking, cycling, or carpooling.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Demand". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Demand

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Equilibrium is a point where planned buying and selling are mutually consistent at a given price.
It is a model result, not a guarantee that markets are fair, instant, or free of power.
cards.example: If many people want concert tickets at a low price, shortage pressure appears until price, quantity, or rules change.
cards.key_takeaway: Equilibrium shows balance between plans, not moral perfection.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Equilibrium shows balance between plans, not moral perfection.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Equilibrium" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If many people want concert tickets at a low price, shortage pressure appears until price, quantity, or rules change.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Equilibrium". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Equilibrium

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Elasticity measures how strongly quantity responds to a change such as price, income, or another price.
It matters because the same tax, shortage, or price increase has different effects when people can easily adjust.
cards.example: Gasoline demand may be less flexible in the short run than restaurant demand because commuting options are limited.
cards.key_takeaway: Elasticity asks how much behavior moves when conditions change.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Elasticity asks how much behavior moves when conditions change.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Elasticity" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Gasoline demand may be less flexible in the short run than restaurant demand because commuting options are limited.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Elasticity". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Elasticity

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Consumer surplus is the gap between what buyers were willing to pay and what they actually pay.
It gives a way to discuss gains from trade without assuming price equals total value.
cards.example: If you would pay 20 dollars for a book and buy it for 12, the extra 8 dollars is surplus to you.
cards.key_takeaway: Value to buyers can exceed the market price they pay.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Value to buyers can exceed the market price they pay.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Consumer surplus" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If you would pay 20 dollars for a book and buy it for 12, the extra 8 dollars is surplus to you.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Consumer surplus". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Consumer surplus

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Producer surplus is the gap between the price sellers receive and the minimum they would accept to supply.
It helps explain why sellers benefit from trades and why cost changes affect participation.
cards.example: A farmer willing to sell apples for 2 dollars per bag gains surplus if the market price is 3 dollars.
cards.key_takeaway: Sellers also receive gains from mutually accepted trades.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Sellers also receive gains from mutually accepted trades.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Producer surplus" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A farmer willing to sell apples for 2 dollars per bag gains surplus if the market price is 3 dollars.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Producer surplus". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Producer surplus

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Price controls are legal limits on prices, such as ceilings that cap how high a price may rise or floors that cap how low it may fall, imposed instead of letting supply and demand set the level.
They may pursue fairness or stability, but they can create shortages, surpluses, quality changes, or rationing when set far from market-clearing levels.
cards.example: A rent ceiling can help current tenants while reducing incentives to add rental housing.
cards.key_takeaway: Changing prices by rule also changes quantities and behavior.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Changing prices by rule also changes quantities and behavior.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Price controls" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A rent ceiling can help current tenants while reducing incentives to add rental housing.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Price controls". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Price controls

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Tax incidence asks who ultimately bears a tax after prices and quantities adjust.
The legal payer is not always the economic bearer because burden shifts toward the side less able to adjust.
cards.example: A payroll tax charged to employers can still lower wages if labor supply and demand conditions permit.
cards.key_takeaway: Who writes the check is not always who bears the cost.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Who writes the check is not always who bears the cost.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Taxes and incidence" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A payroll tax charged to employers can still lower wages if labor supply and demand conditions permit.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Taxes and incidence". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Taxes and incidence

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Prepares for Growth limits and environment.

core_idea: An externality is a cost or benefit of an action that affects others outside the transaction.
Markets can overproduce harmful externalities or underproduce beneficial ones when prices omit spillovers.
cards.example: A factory's pollution affects nearby residents even if they never bought the factory's product.
cards.key_takeaway: When side effects are unpriced, private choices can miss social costs or benefits.

review_prompts.answer_hint: When side effects are unpriced, private choices can miss social costs or benefits.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Externalities" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A factory's pollution affects nearby residents even if they never bought the factory's product.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Externalities". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Externalities

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A public good is hard to exclude people from and one person's use does not greatly reduce another's use.
Those traits create free-rider problems because people can benefit without paying voluntarily.
cards.example: National defense protects everyone in a country's territory, including residents who pay no taxes toward it.
cards.key_takeaway: Some valuable goods are undersupplied when payment cannot be limited to users.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Some valuable goods are undersupplied when payment cannot be limited to users.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Public goods" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: National defense protects everyone in a country's territory, including residents who pay no taxes toward it.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Public goods". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Public goods

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A common resource is one that is difficult to exclude people from using but that can still be depleted, degraded, or crowded as more people draw on it at once.
The problem is overuse unless rules, norms, ownership, or coordination protect the resource.
cards.example: An unmanaged fishery can be exhausted because each boat gains from catching now while depletion is shared.
cards.key_takeaway: Shared access plus depletion creates a governance challenge.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Shared access plus depletion creates a governance challenge.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Common resources" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: An unmanaged fishery can be exhausted because each boat gains from catching now while depletion is shared.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Common resources". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Common resources

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Market power is the ability of a seller or buyer to influence price or terms rather than simply accept them.
It can reduce output, raise prices, shape wages, or limit innovation depending on context.
cards.example: A single broadband provider in a town may charge more than firms facing strong competition.
cards.key_takeaway: Markets need competition or countervailing rules to discipline power.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Markets need competition or countervailing rules to discipline power.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Market power" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A single broadband provider in a town may charge more than firms facing strong competition.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Market power". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Market power

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Information asymmetry appears when one side of a transaction knows much more than the other.
It can cause adverse selection, mistrust, costly screening, warranties, certification, or regulation.
cards.example: A used-car seller may know defects that a buyer cannot easily observe before purchase.
cards.key_takeaway: Unequal information changes what trades happen and at what terms.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Unequal information changes what trades happen and at what terms.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Information asymmetry" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A used-car seller may know defects that a buyer cannot easily observe before purchase.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Information asymmetry". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Information asymmetry

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A principal-agent problem arises when one person acts for another but has different incentives or better information.
Contracts, monitoring, reputation, and ownership can reduce the problem but rarely remove it entirely.
cards.example: A hired manager may prefer short-term bonuses over the owner's long-term health of the firm.
cards.key_takeaway: Delegation needs incentive design because agents have their own goals.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Delegation needs incentive design because agents have their own goals.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Principal-agent problems" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A hired manager may prefer short-term bonuses over the owner's long-term health of the firm.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Principal-agent problems". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Principal-agent problems

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Behavioral biases are predictable patterns where real choices depart from the simplest rational-choice model.
They do not mean people are foolish; they show that attention, framing, habits, and emotions affect decisions.
cards.example: A default retirement contribution can raise saving because inertia shapes behavior.
cards.key_takeaway: Economic actors are human, not frictionless calculators.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Economic actors are human, not frictionless calculators.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Behavioral biases" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A default retirement contribution can raise saving because inertia shapes behavior.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Behavioral biases". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Prepares for Aggregate demand and aggregate supply.

core_idea: Gross domestic product measures the market value of final goods and services produced within an economy over a period.
It is useful for output, income, and spending analysis, but it misses unpaid work, distribution, environmental damage, and many quality differences.
cards.example: Home childcare counts differently from paid childcare even when the care is similar.
cards.key_takeaway: GDP is a powerful output measure with clear blind spots.

review_prompts.answer_hint: GDP is a powerful output measure with clear blind spots.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Measuring output with GDP" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Home childcare counts differently from paid childcare even when the care is similar.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Measuring output with GDP". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.

core_idea: Inflation indexes track price changes for a basket of goods and services over time.
They require choices about weights, quality adjustments, substitution, and whose spending pattern is represented.
cards.example: A retiree and a student may experience different cost changes even under the same national inflation rate.
cards.key_takeaway: Inflation measurement is a designed approximation of changing purchasing power.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Inflation measurement is a designed approximation of changing purchasing power.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Inflation indexes" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A retiree and a student may experience different cost changes even under the same national inflation rate.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Inflation indexes". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.

core_idea: Unemployment measures people without work who are available and actively looking under a specific statistical definition.
It excludes some discouraged, underemployed, unpaid, or informal work situations, so interpretation needs context.
cards.example: Someone who stopped searching may not count as unemployed even if they want a job.
cards.key_takeaway: Unemployment is a defined labor-market measure, not the whole story of work hardship.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Unemployment is a defined labor-market measure, not the whole story of work hardship.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Unemployment" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Someone who stopped searching may not count as unemployed even if they want a job.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Unemployment". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Unemployment

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Labor markets coordinate workers, employers, wages, skills, working conditions, and bargaining power.
They differ from simple commodity markets because jobs involve identity, location, law, training, and long relationships.
cards.example: A nursing shortage may reflect wages, licensing, burnout, training capacity, and regional demand together.
cards.key_takeaway: Work is priced in markets shaped by institutions and human constraints.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Work is priced in markets shaped by institutions and human constraints.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Labor markets" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A nursing shortage may reflect wages, licensing, burnout, training capacity, and regional demand together.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.
Prepares for Growth limits and environment.

core_idea: Productivity measures how much output is produced per unit of input, such as goods created per worker hour, machine hour, or unit of capital invested.
It rises through skills, tools, organization, technology, infrastructure, and better allocation of resources.
cards.example: A warehouse scanner can let the same worker accurately fill more orders per hour.
cards.key_takeaway: Long-run living standards depend heavily on productivity growth.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Long-run living standards depend heavily on productivity growth.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Productivity" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A warehouse scanner can let the same worker accurately fill more orders per hour.

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Human capital is the knowledge, health, skills, and habits that make people more productive or adaptable.
It is built through education, practice, caregiving, nutrition, safety, and work experience, not only formal degrees.
cards.example: Learning to read well changes future job options and civic participation.
cards.key_takeaway: Investing in people is a central economic force.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Investing in people is a central economic force.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Human capital" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Learning to read well changes future job options and civic participation.

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Prepares for Interest rates in the economy.

core_idea: Capital means produced assets used to make other goods and services, while investment creates or improves those assets.
It includes machines, buildings, software, and infrastructure, not just financial assets.
cards.example: A delivery company buying routing software is investing in productive capital.
cards.key_takeaway: Economic investment builds capacity for future production.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Economic investment builds capacity for future production.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Capital and investment" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A delivery company buying routing software is investing in productive capital.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.
Prepares for Interest rates in the economy.

core_idea: Money supports exchange, accounting, and saving, so macroeconomics studies its link to spending and prices.
The same quantity of money can have different effects depending on velocity, banking, expectations, and output capacity.
cards.example: If people hoard extra cash during panic, spending may not rise much.
cards.key_takeaway: Money is central to macro outcomes but works through behavior and institutions.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Money is central to macro outcomes but works through behavior and institutions.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Money in macroeconomics" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If people hoard extra cash during panic, spending may not rise much.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Money in macroeconomics". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Money in macroeconomics

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Interest rates connect present spending with future repayment and influence saving, borrowing, asset prices, and currency values.
They are not one number; different maturities and risks create a structure of rates.
cards.example: A higher mortgage rate can cool housing demand while savings accounts become more attractive.
cards.key_takeaway: Rates are economy-wide prices for time, risk, and liquidity.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Rates are economy-wide prices for time, risk, and liquidity.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Interest rates in the economy" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A higher mortgage rate can cool housing demand while savings accounts become more attractive.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Interest rates in the economy". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Interest rates in the economy

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.

core_idea: Aggregate demand summarizes planned spending across households, firms, government, and foreigners, while aggregate supply summarizes production capacity and costs.
The model helps separate demand-driven slowdowns from supply shocks, though real events often mix both.
cards.example: A pandemic can reduce supply through closures and demand through lost income at the same time.
cards.key_takeaway: Macroeconomic conditions depend on both spending pressure and productive capacity.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Macroeconomic conditions depend on both spending pressure and productive capacity.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Aggregate demand and aggregate supply" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A pandemic can reduce supply through closures and demand through lost income at the same time.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Aggregate demand and aggregate supply". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Aggregate demand and aggregate supply

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Aggregate demand and aggregate supply.

core_idea: Business cycles are expansions and contractions in economic activity around longer-run growth.
They reflect shocks, credit, expectations, policy, inventories, global links, and feedback between spending and income.
cards.example: A drop in demand can lead firms to cut hours, which reduces income and further weakens demand.
cards.key_takeaway: The economy can move in self-reinforcing waves, not just straight lines.

review_prompts.answer_hint: The economy can move in self-reinforcing waves, not just straight lines.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Business cycles" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A drop in demand can lead firms to cut hours, which reduces income and further weakens demand.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Business cycles". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Business cycles

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Aggregate demand and aggregate supply.

core_idea: Fiscal policy uses government spending, taxation, and transfers to affect demand, distribution, and public goods.
It operates through budgets and politics, so timing, targeting, debt, and administrative capacity matter.
cards.example: A temporary unemployment benefit can support household spending during a downturn.
cards.key_takeaway: Fiscal policy changes economic conditions through the public budget.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Fiscal policy changes economic conditions through the public budget.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Fiscal policy" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A temporary unemployment benefit can support household spending during a downturn.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Fiscal policy". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Fiscal policy

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Interest rates in the economy.

core_idea: Monetary policy uses central-bank tools to influence financial conditions and inflation.
It is powerful but indirect because it works through rates, credit, expectations, asset prices, and exchange rates.
cards.example: A rate increase may slow borrowing and reduce inflation pressure after a delay.
cards.key_takeaway: Monetary policy steers the economy through financial channels.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Monetary policy steers the economy through financial channels.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Monetary policy" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A rate increase may slow borrowing and reduce inflation pressure after a delay.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Monetary policy". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Monetary policy

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Trade lets people specialize and exchange across borders, while tariffs tax imports and alter relative prices.
Tariffs can protect some producers but usually raise costs for consumers or downstream firms and invite retaliation.
cards.example: A tariff on imported steel may help steelmakers while raising costs for car manufacturers.
cards.key_takeaway: Trade policy creates winners, losers, and economy-wide ripple effects.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Trade policy creates winners, losers, and economy-wide ripple effects.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Trade and tariffs" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A tariff on imported steel may help steelmakers while raising costs for car manufacturers.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Trade and tariffs". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Trade and tariffs

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Interest rates in the economy.

core_idea: An exchange rate is the price of one currency stated in terms of another, showing how many units of a foreign currency a unit of domestic currency can buy.
It affects import prices, exports, travel, debts, capital flows, and inflation, while responding to rates, expectations, and risk.
cards.example: A weaker domestic currency can make imported electronics more expensive and exports cheaper abroad.
cards.key_takeaway: Currencies connect domestic choices to the world economy.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Currencies connect domestic choices to the world economy.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Exchange rates" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A weaker domestic currency can make imported electronics more expensive and exports cheaper abroad.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Exchange rates". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Exchange rates

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Institutions are formal and informal rules that shape economic behavior, including property rights, courts, norms, and public agencies.
They determine whether contracts can be trusted, investment is safe, and markets can function beyond personal relationships.
cards.example: A land title system can make it easier to borrow, sell, or improve property.
cards.key_takeaway: Economic outcomes depend on rules as well as resources.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Economic outcomes depend on rules as well as resources.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Institutions and property rights" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A land title system can make it easier to borrow, sell, or improve property.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Institutions and property rights". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Institutions and property rights

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Measuring output with GDP.

core_idea: Distribution asks who receives income, wealth, risk, power, and opportunity across an economy.
Efficiency gains can coexist with unequal outcomes, so policy debates often combine economics with values.
cards.example: A policy can raise total output while leaving some workers worse off without compensation.
cards.key_takeaway: An average gain does not tell you who gained.

review_prompts.answer_hint: An average gain does not tell you who gained.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Inequality and distribution" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A policy can raise total output while leaving some workers worse off without compensation.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Inequality and distribution". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Inequality and distribution

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Efficiency describes whether resources are arranged to create more total surplus, while welfare asks about wellbeing and social evaluation.
A situation can be efficient yet unacceptable if distribution, rights, or dignity are ignored.
cards.example: A perfectly priced market for a harmful product may still raise welfare questions.
cards.key_takeaway: Efficiency is useful, but it is not the only social criterion.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Efficiency is useful, but it is not the only social criterion.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Welfare and efficiency" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A perfectly priced market for a harmful product may still raise welfare questions.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Welfare and efficiency". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Welfare and efficiency

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Cost-benefit analysis compares expected gains and losses from a decision in a structured way.
It clarifies tradeoffs but depends on measurement choices, discounting, uncertainty, and whose costs count.
cards.example: A flood barrier analysis must value construction cost, avoided damage, ecological impact, and risk uncertainty.
cards.key_takeaway: Structured comparison improves decisions, but values and assumptions remain visible.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Structured comparison improves decisions, but values and assumptions remain visible.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Cost-benefit analysis" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A flood barrier analysis must value construction cost, avoided damage, ecological impact, and risk uncertainty.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Cost-benefit analysis". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Cost-benefit analysis

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: An economic model is a simplified representation built to clarify one mechanism or question.
Models are useful when their assumptions fit the task and dangerous when treated as complete reality.
cards.example: A supply-and-demand diagram can explain price pressure without capturing every legal or emotional detail.
cards.key_takeaway: Models are maps; judge them by purpose and limits.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Models are maps; judge them by purpose and limits.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Economic models" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A supply-and-demand diagram can explain price pressure without capturing every legal or emotional detail.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Economic models". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Economic models

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Ceteris paribus means holding other things equal so one relationship can be studied clearly.
The phrase protects reasoning inside a model but should not hide that real variables move together.
cards.example: Demand may slope downward holding income constant, even though recessions can change income and prices together.
cards.key_takeaway: Holding other factors constant is a tool, not a claim that the world freezes.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Holding other factors constant is a tool, not a claim that the world freezes.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Ceteris paribus" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Demand may slope downward holding income constant, even though recessions can change income and prices together.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Ceteris paribus". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Ceteris paribus

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Correlation means two things move together, while causation means one produces a change in the other.
Confounders, reverse causality, selection, and timing can make correlations misleading.
cards.example: Ice cream sales and drowning rise in summer, but heat and swimming exposure explain the link.
cards.key_takeaway: Economic evidence must ask what caused what.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Economic evidence must ask what caused what.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Correlation versus causation" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Ice cream sales and drowning rise in summer, but heat and swimming exposure explain the link.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Correlation versus causation". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Correlation versus causation

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Game theory studies strategic situations where each participant's best choice depends on what others do.
It helps explain cooperation, competition, bargaining, coordination, and credible commitments.
cards.example: Two firms deciding whether to cut prices must consider how the rival will respond.
cards.key_takeaway: Strategy matters when outcomes depend on mutual expectations.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Strategy matters when outcomes depend on mutual expectations.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Game theory" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Two firms deciding whether to cut prices must consider how the rival will respond.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Game theory". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Game theory

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.

connections.label: Builds on Aggregate demand and aggregate supply.

core_idea: Expectations are beliefs people hold about future prices, income, or policy that shape the decisions they make today, well before those future events actually occur.
They influence inflation, investment, hiring, saving, exchange rates, and policy effectiveness because people act before outcomes arrive.
cards.example: If firms expect higher future costs, they may raise prices or lock in contracts now.
cards.key_takeaway: The future affects the present through expectations.

review_prompts.answer_hint: The future affects the present through expectations.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Expectations" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If firms expect higher future costs, they may raise prices or lock in contracts now.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Expectations". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Expectations

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: Economic growth depends on resources, technology, institutions, and ecological constraints.
Environmental damage can be an externality, a resource limit, and a distribution issue at the same time.
cards.example: A factory may raise GDP while creating pollution costs not reflected in its sale price.
cards.key_takeaway: Prosperity analysis must include the systems that production draws from.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Prosperity analysis must include the systems that production draws from.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Growth limits and environment" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A factory may raise GDP while creating pollution costs not reflected in its sale price.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Growth limits and environment". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Growth limits and environment

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Economic models simplify reality and policy choices depend on values, data quality, institutions, and current conditions.
core_idea: A strong economic claim names the model, data, mechanism, affected groups, uncertainty, and counterargument.
This habit prevents single charts or slogans from doing more work than they can support.
cards.example: A headline about inflation should prompt questions about time period, measure, cause, and distribution.
cards.key_takeaway: Good economic reading asks what is assumed, measured, and left out.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Good economic reading asks what is assumed, measured, and left out.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Reading economic claims" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A headline about inflation should prompt questions about time period, measure, cause, and distribution.
review_prompts.prompt: Look at the example for "Reading economic claims". What detail does it show that the definition alone would miss?

cards.title: Reading economic claims
